tagline: Calculate your monthly RV loan payment
keywords: rv loan calculator, rv financing, rv monthly payment, motorhome loan, camper financing

faq.question: What is a typical RV loan interest rate?
faq.answer: RV loan rates typically range from 6% to 12% depending on credit score, loan term, and whether the RV is new or used. Excellent credit (750+) can qualify for rates around 6-7%, while lower scores may see 10-12%.

faq.question: How long can I finance an RV?
faq.answer: RV loans commonly range from 5 to 20 years. Loans over $50,000 for new RVs may qualify for terms up to 20 years. Shorter terms mean higher payments but significantly less total interest paid.

faq.question: Should I make a down payment on an RV?
faq.answer: A 10-20% down payment is recommended. It lowers your monthly payment, reduces total interest, and helps avoid being upside-down on the loan since RVs depreciate quickly in the first few years.

formulas: Monthly Payment = P × [r(1+r)^n] / [(1+r)^n - 1] where P = loan amount, r = monthly interest rate, n = total number of payments
Total Interest = (Monthly Payment × n) - P
Loan Amount = Purchase Price - Down Payment

tagline: Estimate fuel costs for your RV road trip
keywords: rv fuel cost calculator, rv gas mileage, rv mpg calculator, motorhome fuel cost, rv trip fuel

faq.question: What is the average MPG for an RV?
faq.answer: Class A motorhomes average 6-10 MPG, Class B vans get 15-25 MPG, Class C motorhomes average 10-16 MPG, and travel trailers depend on the tow vehicle but typically reduce its MPG by 30-50%.

faq.question: Does RV weight affect fuel economy?
faq.answer: Yes, significantly. Every 100 lbs of extra cargo can reduce MPG by about 1-2%. Driving at highway speeds with a fully loaded RV uses considerably more fuel than traveling light.

faq.question: How can I improve my RV's fuel economy?
faq.answer: Drive 55-60 MPH instead of 70+, maintain proper tire pressure, reduce idling, avoid rapid acceleration, travel during low-wind conditions, and keep your RV's engine properly maintained.

formulas: Gallons Needed = Trip Distance / Miles Per Gallon
Fuel Cost = Gallons Needed × Price Per Gallon
Cost Per Mile = Price Per Gallon / Miles Per Gallon

tagline: See how your RV's value changes over time
keywords: rv depreciation calculator, rv resale value, motorhome depreciation, rv value loss, camper depreciation

faq.question: How fast do RVs depreciate?
faq.answer: RVs typically lose 20-25% of their value in the first year, then about 5-8% per year after that. After 5 years, most RVs are worth about 50-60% of their original price. By 10 years, expect 30-40% of original value.

faq.question: Which RV types depreciate the slowest?
faq.answer: Airstream trailers and high-end Class B vans hold value best. Class A diesel pushers depreciate in dollar amount but hold a reasonable percentage. Pop-up campers and entry-level travel trailers depreciate the fastest.

faq.question: Can I slow my RV's depreciation?
faq.answer: Keep mileage low, maintain it meticulously, store it covered or indoors, address repairs promptly, and keep detailed maintenance records. A well-maintained RV can be worth 10-20% more than a neglected one of the same age.

formulas: Year 1 Value = Purchase Price × (1 - First Year Depreciation Rate)
Subsequent Years: Value = Previous Year Value × (1 - Annual Depreciation Rate)
Total Depreciation = Purchase Price - Current Value

tagline: Estimate campsite costs for your RV trip
keywords: rv campground cost, rv park rates, campsite calculator, rv camping budget, rv park fees

faq.question: How much do RV campsites cost per night?
faq.answer: Costs vary widely: BLM/free dispersed camping is $0, national forest sites run $10-25, state parks $20-40, private RV parks $35-75, and resort-style parks $50-150+ per night. Full hookups cost more than dry camping.

faq.question: Are weekly or monthly RV rates cheaper?
faq.answer: Yes, most parks offer weekly discounts of 10-20% and monthly discounts of 30-50%. A $50/night park might charge $300/week or $800/month. Always ask about long-term rates if staying more than a few days.

faq.question: What extra fees should I expect at RV parks?
faq.answer: Common extras include electric metering ($5-15/day for 50 amp), Wi-Fi upgrades ($3-5/day), pet fees ($3-5/day), extra vehicle fees ($5-10/day), and resort amenity fees. Budget an extra 15-20% above the base rate.

formulas: Total Campground Cost = (Nightly Rate × Number of Nights) + Extra Fees Per Night × Nights
With Hookup Surcharge = Base Rate + Hookup Fee Per Night
Average Nightly Cost = Total Cost / Number of Nights

tagline: Calculate cargo capacity and weight limits
keywords: rv weight calculator, rv cargo capacity, gvwr calculator, rv payload, rv weight distribution

faq.question: What is GVWR and why does it matter?
faq.answer: GVWR (Gross Vehicle Weight Rating) is the maximum safe weight of your RV when fully loaded. Exceeding it stresses tires, brakes, suspension, and frame, increasing blowout and accident risk. It is also illegal and voids insurance.

faq.question: How much does water weigh in an RV?
faq.answer: Water weighs 8.34 lbs per gallon. A full fresh water tank (40-100 gallons) adds 334-834 lbs. Many RVers travel with tanks only half full to save weight and fuel.

faq.question: What is CCC vs payload capacity?
faq.answer: CCC (Cargo Carrying Capacity) is the weight available for passengers and cargo after the RV is loaded with full fluids and LP gas. Payload capacity is GVWR minus the dry/unloaded weight. CCC is the more practical number for packing.

formulas: Cargo Carrying Capacity (CCC) = GVWR - Unloaded Vehicle Weight (UVW)
Available Cargo = CCC - Passenger Weight - Water Weight - Propane Weight
Water Weight = Gallons × 8.34 lbs, Propane Weight = Gallons × 4.2 lbs

tagline: Size your solar system for off-grid camping
keywords: rv solar calculator, rv solar panel sizing, boondocking solar, rv off grid power, rv solar setup

faq.question: How many solar panels does an RV need?
faq.answer: Most RVers need 200-800 watts of solar. A minimalist setup (lights, phone charging, small fridge) needs ~200W. A moderate setup (residential fridge, TV, laptop) needs 400-600W. Running AC or heavy loads requires 800W+ plus a generator backup.

faq.question: How many sun hours should I plan for?
faq.answer: Plan for 4-5 peak sun hours per day as a conservative average. The desert Southwest gets 6-7 hours, while the Pacific Northwest may only get 3-4. Winter reduces output by 30-50% compared to summer.

faq.question: What size battery bank do I need with solar?
faq.answer: Your battery bank should store at least 2 days of usage for lead-acid (since you only use 50%) or 1.5 days for lithium (usable to 80-90%). For 100Ah daily use, you need 400Ah lead-acid or 150Ah lithium.

formulas: Daily Wh Needed = Sum of (Device Watts × Hours Used Per Day)
Solar Watts Needed = (Daily Wh / Peak Sun Hours) × 1.25 inefficiency factor
Battery Bank (Ah) = Daily Wh / Battery Voltage / Depth of Discharge

tagline: Estimate your annual RV insurance premium
keywords: rv insurance calculator, rv insurance cost, motorhome insurance, camper insurance estimate, rv coverage cost

faq.question: How much does RV insurance cost?
faq.answer: RV insurance typically costs $1,000-$3,000 per year for motorhomes and $250-$800 for towable RVs. Full-timers pay more (similar to homeowners insurance). Factors include RV value, type, usage, driver age, and location.

faq.question: Do I need full-timer RV insurance?
faq.answer: If you live in your RV more than 6 months per year, you need a full-time RV policy. It adds personal liability and contents coverage similar to homeowners insurance. Standard RV policies may deny claims if you are a full-timer.

faq.question: What does RV insurance cover?
faq.answer: Basic coverage includes liability, collision, and comprehensive. Optional add-ons include full replacement cost, personal belongings, roadside assistance, vacation liability, and total loss replacement. Most policies also cover attached accessories.

formulas: Estimated Premium = Base Rate × RV Type Factor × Age Factor × Usage Factor × Coverage Factor
Base Rate = RV Value × Base Rate Percentage (typically 1.5-3.5% of value)
Full-timer surcharge adds approximately 40-60% to the base premium

tagline: Estimate total cost of your RV road trip
keywords: rv trip cost calculator, rv road trip budget, rv travel planner, rv trip expenses, rv vacation cost

faq.question: How much does an RV trip cost per day?
faq.answer: Budget RV travelers spend $75-150/day, moderate travelers $150-250/day, and luxury travelers $250+/day. This includes fuel, campgrounds, food, and activities. Full-time RVers average $1,500-3,000/month total.

faq.question: What are the biggest RV trip expenses?
faq.answer: Fuel is typically the largest expense (30-40% of total), followed by campground fees (25-35%), food (15-25%), and activities/entertainment (10-15%). Unexpected repairs can also add up — budget a maintenance reserve.

faq.question: How can I reduce RV trip costs?
faq.answer: Use free or cheap camping (BLM, Harvest Hosts, Boondockers Welcome), cook instead of eating out, drive slower to save fuel, get loyalty cards for fuel discounts, and travel during shoulder season for lower campground rates.

formulas: Fuel Cost = (Total Miles / MPG) × Gas Price
Campground Cost = Nightly Rate × Number of Nights
Total Trip Cost = Fuel + Campground + Food Per Day × Days + Activities Per Day × Days

tagline: Find the right generator wattage for your RV
keywords: rv generator calculator, rv generator size, rv wattage calculator, rv power needs, rv generator sizing

faq.question: What size generator do I need for my RV?
faq.answer: A 2,000W generator handles basics (lights, fridge, TV, charging). A 3,000-3,500W handles a 13,500 BTU AC. A 5,500W+ is needed for two ACs or high-draw appliances. Always check your AC's starting watts, which can be 2-3x running watts.

faq.question: What is the difference between starting and running watts?
faq.answer: Running watts is the continuous power needed. Starting watts (surge watts) is the brief spike when a motor starts — typically 2-3x the running watts. Your generator must handle the highest possible starting watts surge.

faq.question: Can I run my RV AC on a portable generator?
faq.answer: A single 13,500 BTU RV AC needs about 1,800 running watts and 2,800 starting watts, so a 3,000W+ generator works. A 15,000 BTU AC needs 3,500W+. Running two ACs requires 5,500W+ minimum. An EasyStart soft starter can reduce starting watts by 60-75%.

formulas: Total Running Watts = Sum of all appliance running wattages
Peak Starting Watts = Total Running Watts + Highest Single Appliance Starting Watt Surge
Recommended Generator = Peak Starting Watts × 1.2 safety margin

tagline: Calculate fuel consumption for your boat trip
keywords: boat fuel calculator, boat gas consumption, marine fuel cost, boat fuel burn rate, boating fuel economy

faq.question: How much fuel does a boat use per hour?
faq.answer: Fuel burn varies enormously. Small outboards (25-75 HP) burn 2-6 GPH, mid-range (150-250 HP) burn 12-25 GPH, and large inboards (300+ HP) burn 20-50+ GPH. A rough estimate is 1 gallon per 10 HP per hour at full throttle.

faq.question: How do I calculate boat fuel consumption?
faq.answer: Use the formula: GPH = (HP × Load Factor) / (Specific Fuel Consumption × 6.1 for gas or 7.1 for diesel). For a quick estimate, multiply horsepower by 0.1 for gallons per hour at full throttle, or 0.06 at cruise speed.

faq.question: What is the one-third fuel rule for boats?
faq.answer: The one-third rule: use one-third of your fuel going out, one-third coming back, and keep one-third in reserve for emergencies. This is a critical safety rule — never plan to use more than two-thirds of your total fuel capacity.

formulas: Fuel Burn Rate (GPH) = Horsepower × Throttle Factor (0.06 cruise, 0.10 WOT) for gasoline
Fuel Needed = GPH × Hours of Operation
Trip Cost = Fuel Needed × Price Per Gallon
Safe Fuel = Total Tank × 2/3 (one-third rule reserve)

tagline: Calculate monthly payments for boat financing
keywords: boat loan calculator, boat financing, marine loan payment, boat payment calculator, yacht financing

faq.question: What are typical boat loan rates?
faq.answer: Boat loan rates typically range from 6-10% for new boats with excellent credit, and 8-15% for used boats. Rates depend on credit score, loan amount, boat age, and loan term. Boats over $100K may qualify for better rates.

faq.question: How long can I finance a boat?
faq.answer: Boat loans commonly range from 5-20 years. Loans under $25K usually max out at 10-12 years. Loans $25-75K can go 12-15 years. Over $75K may qualify for 15-20 year terms. Shorter terms save substantial interest.

faq.question: Is a boat loan tax deductible?
faq.answer: If your boat has a berth, galley, and head (sleeping, cooking, bathroom facilities), it can qualify as a second home. This makes the interest tax-deductible under the mortgage interest deduction, subject to IRS limits.

formulas: Monthly Payment = P × [r(1+r)^n] / [(1+r)^n - 1]
P = Boat Price - Down Payment, r = Annual Rate / 12, n = Years × 12
Total Interest = (Monthly Payment × n) - P

tagline: Calculate anchor line and chain needed
keywords: anchor rode calculator, anchor scope, anchor chain length, anchoring calculator, anchor line needed

faq.question: What scope ratio should I use for anchoring?
faq.answer: The standard scope is 7:1 (7 feet of rode for every 1 foot of depth) for all-rope rode in normal conditions. Use 5:1 for all-chain rode. In storms, increase to 10:1 or more. Never use less than 3:1 — that is emergency-only.

faq.question: How do I calculate the total depth for scope?
faq.answer: Total depth = water depth at high tide + freeboard height (bow to waterline). If the water is 15 feet deep at high tide and your bow is 5 feet above water, your total depth is 20 feet, so 7:1 scope = 140 feet of rode.

faq.question: Should I use chain or rope for anchor rode?
faq.answer: Most boats use a combination: 15-20 feet of chain at the anchor (adds weight for better holding, resists chafe on bottom), then nylon rope for the rest. All-chain rode is best for larger boats and coral/rocky bottoms but is very heavy.

formulas: Total Depth = Water Depth + Tide Rise + Bow Height (freeboard)
Rode Needed = Total Depth × Scope Ratio
Minimum Chain = 1 foot per foot of boat length (rule of thumb)

tagline: Calculate hull speed from waterline length
keywords: boat speed calculator, hull speed calculator, waterline length speed, boat knots calculator, theoretical hull speed

faq.question: What is hull speed?
faq.answer: Hull speed is the maximum efficient speed of a displacement hull, where the bow wave and stern wave wavelength equals the waterline length. Beyond this speed, the boat must climb over its own bow wave, requiring exponentially more power.

faq.question: Can a boat exceed hull speed?
faq.answer: Planing hulls (most powerboats) can exceed hull speed by rising up and riding on top of the water. Displacement hulls (sailboats, trawlers) are practically limited to hull speed. Semi-displacement hulls fall in between.

faq.question: How is hull speed calculated?
faq.answer: Hull speed in knots = 1.34 × square root of waterline length in feet. For example, a 36-foot waterline gives a hull speed of 1.34 × 6 = 8.04 knots. This is based on wave theory and the relationship between speed and wave length.

formulas: Hull Speed (knots) = 1.34 × √(Waterline Length in feet)
Speed (MPH) = Speed (knots) × 1.15078
Speed (km/h) = Speed (knots) × 1.852

tagline: Size your boat's battery bank correctly
keywords: marine battery calculator, boat battery sizing, marine battery bank, boat house battery, marine electrical

faq.question: How do I calculate my boat's electrical needs?
faq.answer: List each electrical device, its amp draw, and hours of daily use. Multiply amps × hours for each to get amp-hours (Ah). Sum all devices for total daily Ah. Common loads: anchor light 1A×10h=10Ah, fridge 5A×12h=60Ah, electronics 3A×8h=24Ah.

faq.question: Should I use AGM or lithium marine batteries?
faq.answer: AGM batteries are cheaper upfront ($200-400 each) but only usable to 50% DoD with 400-800 cycle life. Lithium (LiFePO4) costs 3-4x more but offers 80-100% DoD, 3,000-5,000 cycles, is 60% lighter, and lasts 5-10x longer — often cheaper per cycle.

faq.question: How many batteries do I need on my boat?
faq.answer: Start with daily Ah usage, divide by usable capacity per battery (50% for AGM, 80% for lithium), then add a safety margin. For 200Ah daily use with AGM (100Ah batteries at 50%), you need 200/50 = 4 batteries minimum. Add 1-2 more for reserve.

formulas: Daily Amp-Hours = Sum of (Device Amps × Hours Used Per Day)
Required Battery Capacity (Ah) = Daily Ah / Depth of Discharge
Number of Batteries = Required Capacity / Single Battery Ah Rating
Days of Autonomy = (Battery Bank Ah × DoD) / Daily Ah

tagline: Calculate antifouling paint for your hull
keywords: boat bottom paint calculator, antifouling paint coverage, hull paint calculator, boat paint needed, marine bottom paint

faq.question: How do I estimate hull surface area?
faq.answer: A common approximation: Hull Area (sq ft) = Waterline Length × (Beam + Draft) × 0.85. This accounts for the curved shape of most hulls. For precise numbers, check your boat manufacturer's specs or measure directly in drydock.

faq.question: How many coats of bottom paint do I need?
faq.answer: Apply 2 coats minimum for proper protection, 3 coats on high-wear areas (waterline, keel, rudder, running gear). Each coat should be a different color so you can see when it is time to repaint. More coats = longer protection between haulouts.

faq.question: How often should I repaint my boat bottom?
faq.answer: Most antifouling paint lasts 1-2 seasons for ablative types and 1-3 seasons for hard modified epoxy types. Boats in warm, high-growth waters need annual repainting. Cold-water boats may go 2-3 seasons between applications.

formulas: Hull Surface Area ≈ Waterline Length × (Beam + Draft) × 0.85
Paint Needed (gallons) = (Hull Area × Number of Coats) / Coverage Per Gallon
Typical coverage: 350-450 sq ft per gallon depending on paint type

tagline: Calculate trailer and towing requirements
keywords: boat trailer weight calculator, boat towing calculator, trailer capacity, boat towing requirements, trailer tongue weight

faq.question: How do I calculate total towing weight for my boat?
faq.answer: Total towing weight = boat dry weight + engine weight + fuel weight (6.1 lbs/gal gas, 7.1 lbs/gal diesel) + gear weight + trailer weight. Most trailers weigh 15-25% of their rated capacity. Always stay under your tow vehicle's GCWR.

faq.question: What should the tongue weight be?
faq.answer: Tongue weight should be 10-15% of total trailer weight for proper stability. Too little tongue weight causes trailer sway; too much overloads the tow vehicle's rear axle. Adjust by moving the boat fore or aft on the trailer.

faq.question: What tow vehicle do I need for my boat?
faq.answer: Match your vehicle's tow rating to total weight with a 15-20% safety margin. A 5,000 lb boat+trailer combo needs at least a 6,000 lb tow rating. Consider tongue weight, payload capacity, cooling package, and transmission type too.

formulas: Total Tow Weight = Boat Weight + Engine Weight + Fuel Weight + Gear + Trailer Weight
Fuel Weight = Gallons × 6.1 (gas) or × 7.1 (diesel)
Ideal Tongue Weight = Total Tow Weight × 0.10 to 0.15
Required Tow Rating = Total Tow Weight × 1.15 (safety margin)

tagline: Convert nautical and statute miles and knots
keywords: nautical mile converter, knots to mph, nm to miles, nautical distance converter, knots converter

faq.question: What is a nautical mile?
faq.answer: A nautical mile equals 1.15078 statute miles or 1.852 kilometers. It is defined as one minute of latitude, which makes it naturally convenient for navigation on charts where latitude lines are clearly marked.

faq.question: What is the difference between knots and MPH?
faq.answer: Knots measure speed in nautical miles per hour. 1 knot = 1.15078 MPH. Knots are used in maritime and aviation because they relate directly to latitude degrees, making navigation calculations simpler.

faq.question: Why do sailors use nautical miles instead of regular miles?
faq.answer: Nautical miles correspond directly to degrees of latitude on charts: 1 nautical mile = 1 minute of latitude. This makes distance measurement on nautical charts intuitive — just measure the distance against the latitude scale on the chart edge.

formulas: 1 Nautical Mile = 1.15078 Statute Miles = 1.852 Kilometers
1 Statute Mile = 0.86898 Nautical Miles

tagline: Calculate DIM weight for UPS, FedEx, and USPS
keywords: dimensional weight calculator, dim weight calculator, volumetric weight, ups dim weight, fedex dim weight, usps dim weight

faq.question: What is dimensional weight?
faq.answer: Dimensional weight (DIM weight) is a pricing method that reflects package density. Carriers charge the greater of actual weight or DIM weight. Large, light packages cost more based on size rather than weight. This encourages efficient packaging.

faq.question: What DIM factors do carriers use?
faq.answer: For domestic shipments: UPS and FedEx use a DIM factor of 139 (cubic inches / 139 = DIM lbs). USPS uses 166 for Priority Mail. International shipments typically use 139 or the metric equivalent of 5,000 (cubic cm / 5,000 = DIM kg).

faq.question: How can I reduce dimensional weight charges?
faq.answer: Use the smallest box possible, avoid oversized packaging, use poly mailers for soft goods, consider flat rate options for dense items, and right-size your packaging. Even 1 inch smaller on each dimension can meaningfully reduce DIM weight.

formulas: DIM Weight (lbs) = (Length × Width × Height in inches) / DIM Factor
UPS/FedEx DIM Factor: 139 (domestic), USPS Priority: 166
Billable Weight = MAX(Actual Weight, DIM Weight)
Metric: DIM Weight (kg) = (L × W × H in cm) / 5,000

tagline: Estimate NMFC freight class for LTL shipping
keywords: freight class calculator, nmfc class, ltl freight class, freight density calculator, shipping class estimator

faq.question: What is freight class?
faq.answer: Freight class is an NMFC (National Motor Freight Classification) system ranging from 50 to 500 that standardizes LTL shipping pricing. Lower classes (50-85) are denser, easier to handle goods that cost less to ship. Higher classes (300-500) are light, bulky, or fragile items.

faq.question: How is freight class determined?
faq.answer: The primary factor is density (lbs per cubic foot). Class 50 = 50+ lbs/ft3, Class 70 = 15-22.5 lbs/ft3, Class 100 = 8-10 lbs/ft3, Class 200 = 2-4 lbs/ft3, Class 500 = under 1 lb/ft3. Handling, liability, and stowability also factor in.

faq.question: Why does freight class matter for shipping cost?
faq.answer: Freight class directly determines your LTL shipping rate. A Class 50 shipment might cost half as much as the same weight at Class 100. Accurate classification prevents reclassification fees, which add $50-200+ per shipment when carriers re-weigh and re-measure.

formulas: Density (lbs/ft³) = Weight / (L × W × H / 1,728) where dimensions are in inches
Cubic Feet = (Length × Width × Height in inches) / 1,728
Freight class is determined by density ranges defined by NMFC

tagline: Compare estimated shipping rates by carrier
keywords: shipping cost calculator, shipping rate comparison, ups fedex usps rates, package shipping cost, shipping estimate

faq.question: Which carrier is cheapest for shipping?
faq.answer: USPS is typically cheapest for packages under 1 lb (First Class) and for small, heavy items (Flat Rate). UPS and FedEx are more competitive for heavier packages (10+ lbs) and offer better volume discounts. For very heavy items (70+ lbs), freight carriers are cheapest.

faq.question: How are shipping rates calculated?
faq.answer: Rates depend on: billable weight (greater of actual or DIM weight), distance/zone, service speed, and surcharges. Additional costs include residential delivery ($4-6), fuel surcharge (5-15%), and peak season surcharges. Volume shippers get 20-50% discounts.

faq.question: When should I use flat rate shipping?
faq.answer: USPS Flat Rate boxes are best for dense, heavy items that fit in the box: anything over 4-5 lbs going long distance. A small flat rate box ($10.40) beats weight-based pricing for anything heavy enough. Always compare flat rate vs. regular before choosing.

formulas: Base Rate = Weight × Rate Per Pound × Zone Factor
Total Cost = Base Rate + Fuel Surcharge (% of base) + Residential Fee + Other Surcharges
Zone ranges from 1 (local) to 8 (coast-to-coast), affecting price significantly

tagline: Calculate how many items fit on a pallet
keywords: pallet calculator, pallet loading calculator, boxes per pallet, pallet optimization, pallet stacking calculator

faq.question: What is a standard pallet size?
faq.answer: The most common pallet in North America is the GMA pallet: 48" × 40" (1,219 × 1,016 mm). Other common sizes include 42×42 (telecom), 48×48 (drums), and 40×40 (dairy). The standard max height for a palletized load is 48-60 inches including the pallet.

faq.question: How do I maximize boxes per pallet?
faq.answer: Try different box orientations (length vs width leading). Alternate layer patterns for stability. Consider column stacking for fragile items. Use overhang up to 1 inch if acceptable. Software optimization can increase pallet utilization by 10-20% over manual layouts.

faq.question: What is the max weight for a pallet?
faq.answer: Standard GMA pallets support 2,500 lbs for floor stacking and 2,000 lbs for racked storage. Most carriers limit pallet shipments to 2,500-3,000 lbs per pallet. For heavier loads, use hardwood or reinforced pallets rated for the weight.

formulas: Boxes Per Layer = floor(Pallet Length / Box Length) × floor(Pallet Width / Box Width)
Number of Layers = floor(Max Stack Height / Box Height)
Total Boxes = Boxes Per Layer × Number of Layers
Pallet Utilization = (Boxes Per Layer × Box L × Box W) / (Pallet L × Pallet W) × 100%

tagline: Calculate how many units fit in a container
keywords: container load calculator, container capacity, shipping container calculator, 20ft 40ft container, container loading plan

faq.question: What are standard shipping container sizes?
faq.answer: The most common are 20ft (internal: 19'4" × 7'8" × 7'10", ~1,170 ft3, max 44,000 lbs) and 40ft (internal: 39'5" × 7'8" × 7'10", ~2,390 ft3, max 59,000 lbs). High-cube 40ft containers add 1 foot of height (~2,700 ft3).

faq.question: How many pallets fit in a container?
faq.answer: A 20ft container fits 10 standard pallets (48×40) single loaded or 11 turned sideways. A 40ft container fits 20-21 pallets single loaded. Double stacking pallets doubles capacity if weight allows. Always check weight limits too.

faq.question: Should I use a 20ft or 40ft container?
faq.answer: A 40ft container costs about 25-40% more than a 20ft but offers double the space — much better value per unit if you can fill it. Use a 20ft for heavy, dense cargo that would exceed the 40ft weight limit, or if you cannot fill a 40ft.

formulas: Units Per Row = floor(Container Length / Unit Length)
Rows Per Layer = floor(Container Width / Unit Width)
Layers = floor(Container Height / Unit Height)
Total Units = Units Per Row × Rows Per Layer × Layers (check weight limit)

tagline: Estimate import duties and taxes on goods
keywords: customs duty calculator, import duty calculator, tariff calculator, import tax estimator, customs fees calculator

faq.question: How are customs duties calculated?
faq.answer: Duties are calculated as a percentage of the customs value (usually the transaction value including shipping to the port). The duty rate depends on the product's HS (Harmonized System) code and the country of origin. Rates range from 0% to 25%+ depending on the product.

faq.question: What is the de minimis threshold?
faq.answer: In the US, shipments valued under $800 are generally duty-free (the de minimis threshold). This applies per person per day. Canada's threshold is CAD $20 for duties and CAD $40 for taxes. The EU has eliminated its de minimis for VAT.

faq.question: What additional fees are there besides duties?
faq.answer: Beyond duties, expect: Merchandise Processing Fee (0.3464% of value, min $31.67, max $614.35 for US imports), Harbor Maintenance Fee (0.125% for ocean shipments), customs broker fees ($150-400), and potentially anti-dumping or countervailing duties.

formulas: Duty Amount = Customs Value × Duty Rate %
Customs Value = Product Cost + Shipping to Port + Insurance
MPF (US) = Customs Value × 0.3464% (min $31.67, max $614.35)
Total Import Cost = Customs Value + Duty + MPF + Broker Fees

tagline: Estimate delivery dates by shipping method
keywords: shipping transit time calculator, delivery date estimator, shipping time calculator, package delivery estimate, shipping days calculator

faq.question: How long does ground shipping take?
faq.answer: UPS Ground and FedEx Ground take 1-5 business days depending on distance. Zone 1-2 (local) is typically 1-2 days, while Zone 8 (coast to coast) takes 5 business days. Add 1 day for residential delivery. Saturday/Sunday are not transit days for ground.

faq.question: Does the ship date affect transit time?
faq.answer: Yes. Packages shipped on Friday via ground will not move over the weekend — Monday is the first transit day. Shipping earlier in the week maximizes the chance of same-week delivery. Holiday weeks add delays. Most carriers have a cutoff time (usually 3-5 PM) for same-day pickup.

faq.question: What causes shipping delays?
faq.answer: Common delays: weather events (1-3 days), peak season volume (Dec, Prime Day), incorrect addresses (1-2 days for correction), customs clearance for international (1-5 days), remote/rural delivery areas, and carrier capacity issues.

formulas: Estimated Delivery = Ship Date + Transit Days (business days only)
Business days exclude Saturday, Sunday, and major holidays
Transit days vary by service: Ground 1-5, 3-Day, 2-Day, Next Day

tagline: Calculate shipping insurance cost for packages
keywords: package insurance calculator, shipping insurance cost, parcel insurance, ups insurance, fedex declared value

faq.question: How much does shipping insurance cost?
faq.answer: Carrier insurance (declared value) typically costs $1.05-$3.00 per $100 of value. UPS charges $1.05 per $100 above the included $100 coverage. FedEx is similar. Third-party insurers like Shipsurance or U-PIC offer rates of $0.50-$1.50 per $100, often 40-60% cheaper.

faq.question: Is shipping insurance worth it?
faq.answer: For items over $100, usually yes. The break-even analysis: if your damage/loss rate is 1% and insurance costs 1% of value, it is neutral. But most damage rates are 1-3% for fragile items, making insurance a good value for anything you cannot afford to lose.

faq.question: What does shipping insurance cover?
faq.answer: Standard carrier insurance covers loss, damage, and theft during transit. It typically does NOT cover improper packaging, perishable spoilage, inherent product defects, or delays. Some third-party insurers offer broader coverage including concealed damage.

formulas: Carrier Insurance = ((Declared Value - Included Coverage) / 100) × Rate Per $100
UPS: $100 included free, then $1.05 per additional $100
Third-Party Insurance ≈ Declared Value × 0.8-1.5% (varies by provider)
Break-Even Loss Rate = Insurance Cost / Declared Value × 100
